details.text: Derivative: إِذَا means “when” and is used in Arabic when the future is spoken about. إِذَا is used with both the past and present tense to talk about the future. When the past tense is used to refer to the future then it indicates that you are speaking about something that is bound to happen, as sure as something that happened in the past.

Derivative: كُوِّرَتْ comes from كَوَّرَ – يُكَوِّرُ – تَكْوِير (Takweer) and come from the root letters of ك و ر which literally means to wrap something up. It is used in Arabic in respect to a turban. The idea is of something that is long which is then wrapped around something and closing something off. Here it is in reference to the sun’s light which stretches out which will be wrapped or folded up until there is nothing left to wrap up further i.e., the light will be wrapped back into the sun until the sun loses its light. As such scholars of Tafsir have stated that كُوِّرَتْ means that the sun will become dark whilst others have understood it literally to mean that the sun itself will become folded up and not just its light.

إِذَا الشَّمْسُ كُوِّرَتْ

- It is usual in Arabic for the verb to precede the noun, however, here the noun الشَّمْسُ (the sun) precedes the verb كُوِّرَتْ (will be wrapped or folded up). This is done to highlight the noun and grab the attention of the intended audience through it.
- The use of the passive voice in كُوِّرَتْ (will be wrapped or folded up), where the doer of the action is not mentioned, highlights and draws attention to the idea of the speech and not to the speaker of the idea. It is a style utilised a lot in the first half of this Surah.
Derivative: الصُّحُفُ which means scrolls refers here to the scrolls which record all the deeds of a person.

Derivative: كُشِطَتْ from كَشَطَ which was used to refer to “skinning” the hide or skin off an animal. It could be a reference to the sky turning red like the animal after it has been skinned. The majority of scholars of Tafsir however state that it means that the sky will be stripped away and removed.
Derivative: سُعِّرَتْ from سَعَّرَ which means to kindle a fire so that it rises and blazes high.
Derivative: أُزْلِفَتْ means to be brought close or near out of honour. زُلْفَى means a nearness in terms of an honoured status that is being granted or acknowledged.
Derivative: أَحْضَرَتْ means to bring something forward and present it. It can imply a reluctant form of presentation that someone has been made to bring forward.

عَلِمَتْ نَفْسٌ مَا أَحْضَرَتْ

- This is the answer (جَوَاب - Jawaab) to all the previous conditional statements i.e., “When all these events occur, every soul (or person) will know what it has brought (with it)”.

Derivative: الْخُنَّسِ is the plural of خَانِس and it means planets or stars. It refers to something that proceeds upon a path and then disappears from view i.e., something that both appears and disappears.
Derivative: الْكُنَّسِ comes from كِنَاس which is the hiding place of a deer which it runs to and disappears from view. It refers here to the stars or planets when they disappear from view.

Derivative: الْجَوَارِ comes from جَارَ – جَوْر which means to veer or deviate from the path i.e., a star or planet that veers from its course like a shooting star. It has also been said that الْجَوَارِ is the plural of جَارِيَة (Jaariyah) from the verb جَرَى referring to that which runs, floats or proceeds quickly. It is therefore referring to the stars or planets that move along their course with speed.

Derivative: عَسْعَسَ is from اَسماء الضَّاد (word which mean their opposite). عَسْعَسَ is used for the night when the night is beginning and when it is ending and when the vision is blurry i.e., when the darkness is coming and when it is receding.

Derivative: قَوْلُ (Qawl) means a speech and it differs from كَلَام (Kalaam) which is used when referring to the speech of Allah. كَلَام (Kalaam) refers to speech which is from its original source whilst you can have قَوْل (Qawl - speech) which is not from your own كَلَام (Kalaam) i.e., it can be taken from another source.
Derivative: رَسُولٍ is from the فَعُول form which is a hyperbolised form and refers to someone who delivers something effectively and efficiently and is constantly engaged in delivering.
Derivative: كَرِيمٍ is from كَرُمَ – يَكْرُمُ – كَرَم or كَرامَة and it means nobility and honour.

إِنَّهُ لَقَوْلُ رَسُولٍ كَرِيمٍ

- This verse is the جَواب (Jawaab – answer) to the oaths which were made in the preceding verses.

Derivative: انْكَدَرَتْ from اِنْكَدَرَ – اِنْكِدَار means to lose its colour or brilliance and become dull. The opposite of كَدْرَة (Kadrah) in Arabic is صَفَاء which means pure and crystal clear i.e., without blemish or tinge. انْكَدَرَتْ also carries the meaning in the Arabic language of falling. انْكَدَرَتْ therefore can combine the meanings of losing their light and brilliance and also falling down from the sky.
Derivative: الْعَرْشِ means a throne – عَرَشَ – يَعْرِشُ in classical Arabic referred to the act of building a roof out of wood which was a luxury material or commodity.
Derivative: مَكِينٍ can refer to someone who has a high مَكانَة (Makaanah – status and standing).

Derivative: ثَمَّ (Thamma) can have two meanings in the Arabic language. The first is that it is an adverb of place (ظَرْف المكان – Zharf ul-Makaan) to mean “there” i.e., in that place. Secondly, it can be placed before an adjective to empower and highlight that adjective especially.
Derivative: مُطَاعٍ means to be obeyed and followed and can imply that this obedience is given wilfully and voluntarily.
Derivative: أَمِينٍ means incredibly trustworthy and secure in respect to fulfilling a task or matter.

Derivative: مَجْنُونٍ (insane) comes from the verb جَنَّ which in origin means to cover something up. مَجْنُونٍ (insane) is the one whose intellect, rationale or reasoning has become overshadowed or covered. It can also refer to someone who has been possessed by a Jinn. Insanity and being possessed by a Jinn both imply that the person no longer has an independent functioning intellect.
Derivative: صَاحِبُ means companion and implies familiarity i.e., someone you have lived and interacted with closely.
Derivative: الْأُفُقِ refers to the horizon where the earth and the sky meets in someone’s field of vision.
Derivative: ضَنِينٍ refers to someone who is stingy or sparing with giving information, keeping it back and withholding it.
Derivative: رَجِيمٍ means مَرْجُوم which literally means “stoned or pelted with stones” and implies someone who is “cursed”.

Derivative: شَيْطَانٍ – There are two main opinions regarding the origins of the word شَيْطَانٍ (Shaytan - Satan). The first is that it comes from شَطَنَ which means to be far away from the truth and شَيْطَانٍ would be in the hyperbole form of the فَعال (Fa’aal) form to refer to someone who is “extremely far away from the truth”. The second is that it comes from شَاطَ which means to be engulfed in flames of rage or fuming in rage, in which case شَيْطَانٍ would be from the فَعْلان pattern form.
Derivative: الْعَالَمِينَ refers to creatures of intellect, humans, Jinn and Angels.

Derivative: شَاءَ comes from شَيْء (Shai’) which means something that is concrete and firm. It refers to something that is tangible. شَاءَ (Shaa’a) refers to an intention that is as concrete as a tangible thing.

Derivative: يَسْتَقِيمَ is from اِسْتَقامَ – يَسْتَقِيمُ – اِسْتِقَامَة which means to stand up straight (vertically) or set himself straight. It originates from قَامَ – يَقُومُ which means to stand. اِسْتَقامَ implies the one that wants to stand up straight.

Derivative: سُيِّرَتْ means to be uprooted and made to move from their place as if they are nothing. سَيَّرَ – تَسْيِير comes from سَارَ which means to walk and proceed along casually. A number of Tafsirs explain سُيِّرَتْ to refer to the uprooting of the mountains and their transformation into dust like a mirage i.e., into nothing of any substance.
Derivative: عُطِّلَتْ is the passive of عَطَّلَ which literally means to become useless and redundant i.e., for something to no longer have any use or benefit.

Derivative: الْعِشَارُ is the plural of عُشَراء and refers to she camels which are ten months pregnant and carry that name until they give birth. They represented a great source of wealth, honour, pride and joy amongst the Arabs.
Derivative: الْوُحُوشُ is the plural of وَحْش refers to an animal that does not have affection for other creatures of the land i.e., the wild untamed animal.

Derivative: حُشِرَتْ means to be gathered from حَشَرَ however in origin it is used to refer to herding animals. حَشَرَ also implies that the gathering is a concentrated form of gathering i.e., to be packed together.

Derivative: سُجِّرَتْ is from سَجَّرَ – تَسْجِير means to heat something up and make it go ablaze. It can include within its meaning that which makes it heat up and become ablaze i.e., like the fuel of the fire. تَسْجِير also means to make or cause something to overflow. In relation to the seas and oceans, it has been understood to mean that they will be set ablaze or heated to the point that they boil or burst over, overflow and cross their boundaries.

Derivative: الْبِحَارُ – It is the plural of بَحْر which means the sea or ocean. أَبْحُر (Abhur) is the plural that indicates a smaller amount (جَمْعُ القِلَّة) whilst بِحَار (Bihaar) is the plural that indicates plenty (جَمْعُ الكَثْرَة) and as such الْبِحَارُ refers to all of the seas and oceans.

Derivative: النُّفُوسُ is the plural of plenty (جَمْعُ الكَثْرَة) for نَفْس (Nafs) which refers to a person or a soul as a reference to a person. النُّفُوسُ therefore refers to all the souls or people on mass. Additionally, the نَفْس (Nafs) has been mentioned in the Qur’an as something that moves through different states or fluctuations which is exposed to different factors and can be understood to refer to the inner self and that part or aspect of a person that he has to struggle with to keep in order.

Derivative: زُوِّجَتْ is the passive voice verb form of زَوَّجَ – تَزْوِيج and it means to pair together or to pair off. It can refer to a number of pairings that take place in relation to the Day of Judgement.

Derivative: الْمَوْءُودَةُ is an Ism ul-Maf’ool (اِسمُ المَفْعُول – Objective noun) from وَأَدَ – يَئِدُ - وَأْدًا and it means to bury someone alive. الْمَوْءُودَةُ means the female that was buried alive.
Derivative: ذَنْبٍ means a sin or crime and can refer to the slightest infraction or offence.
